content: Research demonstrates that omega-3 fatty acids exert a multitude of effects on health and aging, including inflammation, cardiovascular, neurocognitive, musculoskeletal, immunological, and respiratory health.
Omega-3 fatty acids that are found as over-the-counter supplements and prescription medications come in a variety of forms, including triglyceride and phospholipid.
A possible confounding variable of certain omega-3 clinical trials and their results may be the use of potentially unreliable biomarkers, a factor that may produce hard-to-interpret results.
A person's omega-3 status varies depending on several factors, including genetics, diet, gender, and age and may not be reflective of intake.

Research has revealed that omega-3 fatty acids are involved in nearly every aspect of human physiology, exerting a wide range of effects on multiple organ systems and influencing cardiovascular, neurocognitive, musculoskeletal, immunological and respiratory health.
Emerging evidence indicates that resolving inflammation may be equally important for maintaining good health.
A randomized, controlled intervention trial included 138 sedentary adults with overweight who were between 40 and 85 years old.
The participants received daily omega-3 supplements or a placebo for four months.
Before and after the intervention, the participants took a test to gauge social stress.

The participants provided blood and saliva samples as a means to measure cortisol (a stress hormone), telomerase; anti-inflammatory cytokines, including interleukin (IL)-100 (IL-10); and proinflammatory cytokines, including IL-6, IL-12, and tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNFalpha).
